No matching skill found. Here's a villain monologue:

---

You call me the villain. How flattering. How *lazy*.

Let me tell you what I saw twenty years ago: a city rotting from the inside, run by men in tailored suits who'd never missed a meal deciding which neighborhoods got the hospital and which got the parking lot. I saw the math. I ran it, actually — spreadsheets, projections, fifty years of data. The system was eating itself. And I was the only one willing to say it out loud.

I went to the council. I went to the press. I wrote the report that everyone cited and no one read. You know what changed? *Nothing.* Because the people with the power to change things were the same people benefiting from the rot.

So yes. I took matters into my own hands. I moved money, I made deals, I did things that looked *ugly* from the outside. And now there's a hospital in Marlowe Heights. There's clean water in the Flats. There are three thousand people alive who your "heroes" had already written off as acceptable losses.

Every person who calls me a monster is standing on something I built.

The difference between a villain and a visionary is just *patience* — and whether you won.

I didn't win. Not yet.

But I will.

---

Leans into the "ends justify means" logic without making it cartoonish — the character genuinely believes it, which makes it work. Want me to adjust the tone, setting, or stakes?

---

**"The Weight of Mercy"**

You want to call me a monster? Fine. Say it. I've heard it from everyone who couldn't face what I had to face.

I watched this city rot for thirty years. Thirty years of good people — *good* people — mugged on the same corner, same night, every summer, because the precinct was bought and the DA was bought and the city council was bought, and everyone *knew* it, and everyone kept electing the same smiling faces anyway. You know what that is? That's not corruption. That's a choice. A collective, conscious, cowardly choice to keep suffering as long as the suffering happened to someone else.

I didn't make that choice.

I built something. I organized something. And yes — *yes* — people got hurt. People who were going to hurt more people, for more decades, until their sons took over and hurt people after them. I ended cycles. That's not cruelty, that's *surgery*. You don't weep for the tumor.

And here you are. Standing in the ashes of the machine I burned down, outraged. Genuinely outraged. You know what that tells me? You were comfortable. The machine was working *for* you. The ones who cry loudest about how I did it are always the ones who were fine with how it was.

I'm not asking you to love me. History never loved the people who did what had to be done. Caesar. Cromwell. Every man who ever stood in the gap between what *is* and what should be.

They called them monsters too.

They called them heroes later.

I'll wait.
